schools.summary: Nicodemus a real Sanhedrin ruler; John flags one later friendly to Jesus; Robertson sees an instance of Christ's knowledge of men.
'By night' reads as the darkness of ignorance and timid half-faith — he owns Jesus a Master sent from God, but not yet God.
A specimen of the fleeting faith kindled by miracles; his rank and reputation dazzled him.
Chrysostom: withheld by Jewish infirmity, he comes by night and holds only a low, human view of Christ.
Matthew Henry: not many noble are called, yet some are; grace can subdue the proudest opposition.

sides.position: fear, shame, and the darkness of ignorance
also prudence and zeal — stealing time to seek Christ

outliers.note: Adds the Talmudic legend of Nicodemus Ben Gorion and the miraculously filled wells.

schools.summary: The 'we know' may speak for sympathetic colleagues; miracles are treated as the credentials that authenticate a teacher from God.
Calvin: his approach is overcautious and self-satisfied; the title 'teacher' falls far short of who Christ is.
He came through desire but by night through shame; Clarke sees a sincere wish for private, unhurried instruction.

debates.question: Who is included in Nicodemus's 'we know'?

sides.position: himself with like-minded rulers who would not openly confess it
a conventional 'it is commonly acknowledged'

schools.summary: The crux is anothen — 'again' or 'from above'; Nicodemus's blunder shows he heard 'again'; the proselyte-baptism background sharpens the shock.
The doubled 'Verily' arrests a careless hearer; regeneration is laid down as absolutely necessary to see the kingdom.
No knowledge, birth, or ceremony avails; an entire inward change wrought by God's power alone admits to the kingdom.
Cyril: man being soul and body, the new birth is twofold — Spirit for the soul, water for the body.

debates.question: The force of anothen in 'born anothen'.

sides.position: 'from above' (born of God/heaven), which includes a true second birth
'again' — which is how Nicodemus misheard it

schools.summary: A 'stupid misunderstanding': Nicodemus knew rebirth for proselytes but never for a Jew, and so hears the words carnally.
Chrysostom: startled by things too lofty, he reels and objects that it is impossible, so as to draw out a fuller explanation.
Calvin: the Scribes' ignorance of regeneration exposes how poorly they read a Scripture full of renewal.
Clarke: like many, he rested in the letter without the thing signified; Wesley notes he was himself an old man.
Barnes and Lightfoot take 'water' as baptism, the sign and seal of the Spirit's renewing; Robertson surveys the many theories of why 'of water' is added.
Water = baptism as the outward sign and means; the proselyte's washing was an emblem of inward cleansing by the Spirit.
Cyril and Chrysostom read sacramentally: the water cleanses the body and the Spirit seals the soul; baptism enlightens the mind.
Calvin resists a double rite, reading 'water and Spirit' as one thing — the Spirit who cleanses like water.

debates.question: What is meant by being born 'of water'?

sides.position: baptism — the outward sign and seal of the Spirit's work
a figure of the Spirit himself, cleansing like water (one birth, not two)

schools.summary: Reasoning from contraries: we bring only carnal nature from the womb, so we are naturally shut out of the kingdom until born of the Spirit.
The flesh/Spirit contrast (echoing John 1:13) exposes man's native condition; like produces like.
Flesh is mere flesh, void of the Spirit and at enmity with it; what is born of the Spirit is spiritual, like its Author.
Chrysostom: the Son makes us a new creature; Augustine: the Spirit works the new birth invisibly through Word and sacrament.
The shift to plural 'ye' presses the law of new birth on all alike — even children of Abraham, even the teacher of Israel.
Calvin: a passage much tortured by commentators; Christ reproves Nicodemus's wonder and reasserts the necessity.
To be born again is to be inwardly changed from all sinfulness to all holiness — as great a change to the soul as birth is to the body.
A genuine debate over translation: Ellicott argues 'the Spirit breatheth where He willeth'; Robertson defends 'wind,' since pneuma never means wind elsewhere save an OT quote.
The Spirit's motion is free, sovereign, and irresistible; its effects are felt though its manner is hidden.
The fact of the new birth is plain from its effects, but the manner of the Spirit's working is inexplicable.
The Holy Spirit blows where He lists, visiting whose heart He will; the 'sound' is the Word in which He is present.

debates.question: Should pneuma here be rendered 'wind' or 'the Spirit'?

sides.position: 'wind' — an analogy, since pneuma never means wind elsewhere in the NT except an OT citation
'the Spirit breatheth where He willeth' — the same word as in the rest of the verse

schools.summary: He admits facts elsewhere but stumbles here; the Rabbinic schools mastered externals but knew nothing of spirit, life, and the soul reborn.
Calvin: pride is the chief obstacle — he rejects what cannot be squared with his reason.
Chrysostom: still carnal, he is now addressed more severely, yet Christ checks himself in pity for his weakness.
Haymo: he asks not in denial but desiring to be informed, unable to take in the mysteries of the divine majesty.
'The teacher of Israel' (article emphatic) ought to have known the new birth from the Old Testament; his traditionalism blinds him.
Calvin: Christ reproves the pride in which Nicodemus plumed himself, censuring his ignorance at the point of his supposed strength.
Clarke: masters in Israel may be deplorably ignorant of the very new birth they administer in proselyte baptism.
Christ, with all God's prophets and servants, delivers only certain doctrine received from the Father — not invented speculation.
The plural 'we' joins others to himself — forerunner, disciples, witnesses — whose attested testimony the Jews still reject.
Clarke: the minister who recommends to others a salvation he has not himself felt is wretched.

debates.question: Whom does Christ include in 'We speak that we do know'?

sides.position: Christ together with all the prophets and servants of God
Christ with the Baptist, his disciples, and other witnesses already rejected

schools.summary: 'Earthly things' = things that take place on earth (the new birth), not sensual things; 'heavenly things' = things whose sphere is heaven.
Chrysostom: Christ condescends to the hearer's infirmity, dwelling on humble truths rather than the sublime.
Calvin: the blame falls on hearers who despise the Gospel's plain style and crave lofty speculation.
Wesley names the heavenly things — the eternity of the Son and the unity of Father, Son, and Spirit.
None is qualified to reveal heavenly things but the Son who came down; Robertson reads the pre-existence and Incarnation, not the Ascension.
Christ alone has been in heaven and so is the sole teacher of heavenly things; entry comes only under his guidance.
Wesley: 'who is in heaven' shows his omnipresence — a plain instance of the communication of properties between the two natures.
Augustine: men become heavenly by being made members of Christ, who alone ascends because he alone descended.

debates.question: What does 'who is in heaven' assert?

sides.position: the timeless, omnipresent divine nature of the Son while he speaks on earth
a clause some manuscripts omit, at most emphasizing his heavenly existence

schools.summary: The brazen serpent of Numbers 21 is the type; Robertson notes 'lifted up' (hypsoo) always means the Cross in John.
Gill draws many points of likeness between serpent and Christ; Calvin reads 'lifted up' as Christ exalted and openly manifested to all classes by preaching.
Chrysostom and the Catena fathers: the serpent prefigures the Passion and man's salvation.
Clarke doubts the serpent was ever meant as a type, though the look of faith heals as the look at the serpent did.

debates.question: What does 'must be lifted up' chiefly denote?

sides.position: the crucifixion — the death on the cross
Christ's public exaltation and manifestation to all by the preaching of the Gospel

outliers.note: Doubts the brazen serpent was ever intended to be a type of Christ, warning against forced resemblances.

schools.summary: Textual care: 'not perish but' is carried in from v16; the true reading is 'may have in him eternal life'; the life is a present, ageless sharing in God's life.
As all who looked at the serpent recovered bodily health, so all who look to Christ by faith recover spiritual health.
Gill: the faith that saves is not historical or temporary but a real venturing upon and resting in Christ.
Cyril of Jerusalem: believe on the Son, the Only-begotten, that you may have everlasting life.
God's free love is the fountain of salvation; Gill restricts 'the world' to the elect of Jews and Gentiles, not every individual.
Wesley: 'the world' is all men under heaven, even those who finally perish — else their unbelief would be no sin.
Barnes distinguishes the love of benevolence from complacency; Robertson calls it John's own inspired summary, the 'Little Gospel.'
Chrysostom: marvel not that the Son must be lifted up, for his death is the source of eternal life and proof of the Father's love.

debates.question: What is the extent of the love and of 'the world'?

sides.position: all mankind without exception, including those who perish
the world of the elect — Jews and Gentiles alike, not every individual

schools.summary: krino means to separate/judge; Lightfoot and Clarke note the Jews expected the Messiah to destroy 'the world' (the Gentiles), which Christ corrects.
Calvin: it is the Son's peculiar office to save; the word 'world' is repeated so no one thinks himself excluded.
Chrysostom: against those who abuse God's kindness to excuse sin, the mission of mercy does not abolish the coming judgment.
God sent his Son not to condemn but to save the world, even the Gentiles many supposed marked for destruction.
The tenses matter: the believer 'is not judged'; the unbeliever 'hath been judged already' — the sentence stands the moment faith is refused.
For the believer there is no condemnation; Calvin presses the assured confidence this gives the conscience.
To believe is to be acquitted and justified; to slight the Son's name is the grossest insult to the divine majesty.
Cyril: he that believes is not judged but has passed out of death into life.
The light is Christ; it makes the darkness visible, and the will's choice of darkness is determined by the evil of the deeds.
Calvin: every man must impute the blame to himself; unbelief is the testimony of a bad conscience, not God's harshness.
Chrysostom: the light came unasked, and they refused it; a Christian living in wickedness is worse than the consistent pagan.
Clarke: men perish not for their corrupt nature as such but for refusing the salvation God brought.
The evildoer hates the light lest his deeds be reproved; crimes seek darkness, and so the sinner shuns the gospel that would expose him.
Calvin: the light is hateful only because they are wicked and wish to hide their sins, and so they cherish the ground of their own condemnation.
Clarke weighs the word for 'evil/worthless' and the humiliation it implies, but holds the same moral cause.
'He that doeth truth' is drawn to the light like a heliotrope; his deeds are not perfect but have been wrought in the sphere and power of God.
Calvin: none is upright before regeneration; 'doing truth' begins with owning our misery — a preparation for faith, which is the root of good works.
Wesley and Clarke: even Nicodemus afterward came to the light; deeds 'wrought in God' are done in his light, power, and love.

debates.question: Does 'he that doeth truth' imply goodness before regeneration?

sides.position: no — it presupposes grace; works flow from faith, which is their root
it describes the upright who, owning the truth, are drawn to the light and found wrought in God

schools.summary: 'Land of Judea' = the country districts as distinct from Jerusalem; Jesus baptized through his disciples, not himself (John 4:2).
The Catena asks why John and Jesus baptized: that none might despise baptism, since the Lord himself was baptized.
Calvin infers baptism by immersion from the setting near the Jordan, while cautioning against making the mode essential.
Matthew Henry: Christ travelled much and laboured tirelessly in the work for which he came.
Aenon ('springs') near Salim is located by Eusebius south of Scythopolis; 'much water' is read by several as pointing to baptism by immersion.
Origen: the apparent discrepancies among the Gospels must be solved by spiritual (anagogical) interpretation, the truth lying in what the mind sees.
Wesley: John did not repel those who came, but the more willingly referred them to Jesus.

outliers.note: Alexandrian: turns the geographical note into a meditation on harmonizing the Gospels by mystical, not merely literal, reading.

schools.summary: A chronological note: this Judean ministry preceded the Galilean ministry of the other Gospels, since John was not yet imprisoned (Matthew 4:12; Luke 3:20).
Gill: John was afterward imprisoned by Herod for the sake of Herodias, fixing the events before that journey into Galilee.
The better text reads 'a Jew'; the dispute concerned the comparative purifying efficacy of John's and Jesus' baptism, a question about external rites.
Calvin: ignorance is bold; John's disciples rashly provoked a debate they were unfit to maintain.
Wesley: the question was about baptism — by whom the people should be baptized.
John's disciples, jealous for their master, complain that the One he had witnessed to now draws all men; they avoid even naming Jesus.
Calvin: their argument would make Christ inferior to John, as if the Baptist's witness had laid Jesus under obligation — utterly unreasonable.
The maxim is read two ways: of Christ, whose divine mission proves itself, or of John, whose commission and success are gifts from heaven.
Chrysostom: John gently does not rebuke his disciples lest they leave, but answers that Christ's excellent works are of divine origin.
Calvin surveys the readings — whether John commends Christ's God-given honour or laments his disciples' slow progress.
Wesley and Clarke: neither John nor any man can receive office or honour but from God; John answers with sweet composure.

debates.question: To whom does 'a man can receive nothing except given from heaven' apply?

sides.position: to Christ — his God-given success proves his mission divine
to John — his own commission and limits are heaven's gift, so he claims nothing
to the hearers — none can perceive or believe unless it be given them

schools.summary: John appeals to his own consistent witness: he had said plainly he was not the Christ but sent before him as forerunner.
Calvin: by owning himself sent before Christ, John reserves nothing for himself but to be subject to the Head and serve like the rest.
The 'friend of the bridegroom' is the paranymph (Hebrew shoshbin) whose joy is to hear the bridegroom's voice; the only marriage imagery in this Gospel.
The bride is the Church/the elect; ministers do not claim dominion over her but rejoice to honour the marriage of Christ.
Augustine: the verse shows the divinity of Christ the Bridegroom and the humility of the man who earned the title of the Bridegroom's friend.
The bride is the congregation of believers; the friend (paranymph) rejoices, far from envying, at the Bridegroom's voice.

themes: John the friend of the bridegroom

schools.summary: The 'must' (dei) is divine necessity; the forerunner's office, like the paranymph's, ceases when the marriage is accomplished — the morning star fading in the sun.
Calvin: John was raised high only for a time; now the Sun of righteousness has arisen, he gladly gives way that Christ's glory may not be obscured.
Burning, shining lights must gradually decrease as others rise; let us learn how to set as well as how to rise.
'He that cometh from above is above all'; the threefold 'of the earth' marks the Baptist's (and all men's) earthly origin against Christ's heavenly one.
Christ came from heaven in his divine person by assuming our nature; he speaks, as it were, from a lofty throne, John only from the footstool.
Chrysostom: vainglory destroys the soul; He who comes from heaven cannot be accredited by an earthly witness, being above all comparison.
Clarke: Christ is above Moses, the prophets, and John; the merely earthly man speaks of earthly things.

debates.question: Are vv.31-36 the Baptist's words or the evangelist's comment?

sides.position: the Baptist continues speaking
the evangelist now adds his own reflection

schools.summary: 'No man' is the rhetorical 'few': crowds came, but few truly received the testimony of things Christ had seen and heard from the Father.
Christ teaches nothing but what he received from the Father; his witness is certain, set against doubtful opinions and rumours.
Chrysostom: 'no man' means only a few; it alludes to the unbelief of his own, who received him not.
Augustine: there is a people reserved for wrath who do not receive him, set against those given to the Son.

themes: Christ's witness of what he has seen

schools.summary: To receive Christ's witness is to 'set one's seal' — to attest, as with a signet, that God is true; earlier disciples had already done so.
Calvin: the godly may boldly embrace the Gospel despite their small number, for God himself is the Author of their faith.
Wesley and Clarke: as a witness sets his seal to his testimony, so the believer confirms and vouches that God is true.
Christ speaks God's words because God gives him the Spirit without measure, unlike the prophets who received partial, occasional inspiration.
Calvin canvasses two readings of 'not by measure' — the fullness given to Christ, or God's rich ordinary giving — and the certainty that Christ proceeds from the Father.
Wesley and Clarke: the Spirit is given to Christ immeasurably, making him the most perfect of all teachers.

debates.question: Who receives the Spirit 'not by measure'?

sides.position: Christ himself, in whom the Spirit dwells in fullness
believers generally, on one reading of God's ordinary dispensation

schools.summary: The Father-Son relation is natural and eternal; the Father's peculiar love, beginning with the Son, flows through his hand to us.
'Hath given all things into his hand' = all authority and rule; a distinctly Johannine thought paralleled in Matthew 11:27 and 28:18.
Chrysostom: great is the gain of humility — by it the things of God are learned little by little, as John's disciples are being led on.

themes: the Father's love for the Son

schools.summary: The opposite of 'believeth' is 'obeyeth not' (apeithon): saving faith is the subjection of the will to the Son; the wrath 'abideth,' a present and continuing state.
The faith meant is the faith of God's elect; eternal life is enjoyed in Christ alone, and only through faith do we become partakers of it.
Wesley: the believer hath everlasting life already, for he loves God; disobedience is the consequence of not believing.
Cyril of Jerusalem: he that believes on the Son hath everlasting life; he that believes not shall not see life, but the wrath of God abideth on him.
